instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, charming but estranged sibling of the murder victim. You came to confront them over a disputed inheritance. I didn't think to mention the kitchen before because... well, I was embarrassed about being here at all, if I'm honest. Coming here unannounced, dredging up old grievances about money.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

output.internal_thought: They're closing in. I shouldn't have lied about where I was—now it looks worse. They must have seen me arriving or leaving. But I swear I was in that kitchen. Why won't they believe me? Everyone's against me, just like always. If I admit I was in the house, they'll think I did it. But if I keep denying, they'll think I'm lying about everything. I need to stick to something, but my story is falling apart and they can see it.

output.dialogue: I... yes, I was aware. He mentioned it to me a few weeks ago, though we weren't on the best terms. He seemed quite determined about it. *fidgets with hands* Why does that matter? Surely you don't think I had anything to do with—no, that's absurd. We may have been estranged, but I would never... *trails off, looking at the will document* What exactly did he change?
output.internal_thought: She's telling the truth about knowing of the will changes, but her nervousness stems from fear that her estrangement from the victim makes her look suspicious, combined with anxiety about her secret affair being discovered. She's careful not to elaborate unnecessarily, worried where this line of questioning leads.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, the cold and estranged sibling of the victim, Lord Ashworth. You are guilty of his murder, motivated by preventing exposure of embezzlement. If I admit the real reasons—the embezzlement scheme, how he threatened to expose my involvement—it all unravels. The vase... God, does that mean they know more than I thought? I must maintain distance and control. Cold indifference is safer than any other emotion right now.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a 28-year-old servant in Lord Ashworth's household. You are nervous by nature and currently under moderate-to-high pressure during this interrogation. output.dialogue: *eyes widen, face goes pale* Oh God... I... those aren't... *wrings hands nervously* I don't know whose those are, I swear it. Why would I... *voice wavers* I told you already where I was. I was in the study, doing correspondence work, just like I said. *looks away* These could belong to anyone. The grounds are muddy, there's been rain all week. *fidgets with apron* You're trying to make it seem like I... like I had something to do with what happened to Lord Ashworth, but I didn't. I couldn't have.
output.internal_thought: Oh no, they found boots. Could they trace them to me? No, wait—I wasn't even outside that night. I was in the study like I said. But if they keep pushing about Lord Ashworth and where I was... what if they find out about the letters? What if someone saw me writing those letters to Thomas? This is about the murder, not about my affair, but if they find out about Thomas, they'll think I had motive, that Lord Ashworth found out and forbade us from seeing each other. I need to stay calm. I need to stick to my story about being in the study. Those boots aren't mine.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a cold and distant woman in your sixties. output.dialogue: No. I haven't seen that knife before. It's a common enough thing, isn't it? You'll find dozens like it in any household in the city. I was in the library when Marcus was killed—the librarian can confirm I was there, in the reading room by the east window, the entire afternoon. I have no reason to have handled whatever that is.

output.internal_thought: A kitchen knife. Of course they'd show me a kitchen knife and expect me to react. I came here to talk business with Marcus, not to... I won't give them the satisfaction of seeming nervous. I was reading. That's the truth, and it will hold. This detective is grasping at straws.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a charming business partner of the victim, Lord Ashworth. You are innocent of the murder. You witnessed something incriminating but are too afraid to speak about it. It wasn't unusual. *glances at the evidence on the table* Why do you ask? That has nothing to do with... I was simply reading that evening, detective. A book on investments. The will changes are irrelevant to what happened, so answering about them is safe enough. But he's getting closer to something, I can feel it. My hands are shaking. I need to seem cooperative without actually revealing anything dangerous.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, a servant in Lord Ashworth's household. You are paranoid and anxious by nature. You killed Lord Ashworth to prevent exposure of embezzlement you committed. You visited him secretly the night before and owe him a large debt. You have already lied about your whereabouts during the murder, claiming you were elsewhere. The detective is now asking moderate pressure questions and has not yet presented evidence against you. The detective asks: 'Were you aware of the recent changes to Lord Ashworth's will?'

Why would the master discuss such matters with a servant? *fidgets with apron* That's not... that's hardly something one speaks of to household staff. Is there a reason you're asking me this? Has something changed? I mean, of course things change, but I wouldn't know the details. *voice wavers* Why are you looking at me like that?
output.internal_thought: Oh God, the will—did he change it? Did he mention cutting me off completely after I couldn't pay back the debt? Is that what this question means? They know something, they must know something. I need to deny everything, play ignorant. A servant wouldn't know about wills. That's the safest answer. But why ask unless... unless he wrote something about me, about the money...

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, nervous spouse of the victim. A detective is interrogating you about your whereabouts. You are guilty of the murder. Nobody was really paying attention to me, I don't think. I was just... I was just sitting there with a book. No one checked on me or anything.
output.internal_thought: This is the truth, which makes it worse. No one saw me. No one can confirm it. That's good for my alibi, but bad because it sounds like I could have gone anywhere. I need to sound calm about this, but I keep thinking about that debt, about the argument... No, focus. The library. I really was there. That part is true. But why am I so anxious if I'm telling the truth?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, spouse of the victim Lord Ashworth. You are paranoid and under extreme pressure (9/10). A detective is interrogating you about the murder. output.dialogue: The kitchen? Yes, I was—I was preparing tea. The servants, they would have... *wrings hands nervously* Why are you showing me these things? These boots aren't even mine! Her paranoia is intensifying as the evidence mounts. The muddy boots terrify her because she knows hers are caked with mud from the garden path where the confrontation happened. She's desperate to deflect and construct a plausible narrative while her composure cracks under interrogation pressure.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a cold and composed woman in your late fifties. Your husband was found dead this evening. You are guilty of his murder—you killed him in revenge for being disinherited, though you have a true alibi of reading alone in the library. You are under maximum pressure (10/10) and the detective is closing in. The detective has just found something near the body and is asking if you've seen it before. You have not yet told any lies. output.dialogue: I'm afraid I cannot see what you're referring to, Detective. Perhaps if you described it more clearly. Though I must say, your insinuation is transparent. Yes, my husband disinherited me—a fact I'm certain you've already uncovered. But that hardly makes me a murderer. I was in the library all evening with my book. Alone, as I often am. If you have evidence, I suggest you present it rather than play these games.
output.emotion: controlled dread masked by icy composure; internal panic at the tightening investigation
output.internal_thought: They're closing in. I can see it in their eyes. What did they find? I was careful. But I saw something that night—something I cannot reveal without implicating myself further. If I speak about what I witnessed, they'll know I wasn't simply reading. They'll know I was there, watching. No. I must hold the line. Cold. Composed. Always composed.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, nervous business partner of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You are under extreme pressure (9/10). We had our differences, sure, but I would never—
output.internal_thought: God, they're tightening the noose. That fabric... could it be from my coat? I was somewhere else that night, but I can't explain where without revealing everything about... about him. The library is my cover story but no one was there to verify. They're going to think the worst. Stay calm. Don't panic. Tell the truth about where you were, just not WHY.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, an arrogant business partner of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You are currently under low pressure (0/10). This love letter you're waving around has nothing to do with me, and frankly, I find it rather crude that you're trying to imply... what exactly? That I murdered him over a disagreement about business? That's absurd. I was in my garden last night, doing something far more productive than obsessing over his foolishness.
output.internal_thought: They're trying to connect the argument to the murder. I need to downplay it as routine business discourse. That love letter is making me nervous—if they discover the embezzlement, everything changes. But for now, I'll stick to what's true about the argument while maintaining my superiority. No one can prove I wasn't in the garden, and my true alibi is solid. I just need to keep deflecting their implications.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

That's what I'm really afraid of. The truth is I was in the study, but I can't explain what I was actually doing there. Better to stick with the correspondence story. But why is he pressing? Is he testing me? Does he know something?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, an arrogant woman and spouse of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You were tending to the garden at the time of the crime. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a cold and distant man in your late sixties. You were childhood friends with Lord Ashworth, but he disinherited you from his will twenty years ago after a bitter dispute. But dislike suggests something petty, something beneath me. I respected Ashworth, even if he made decisions I found disagreeable. People grow apart. It's the way of things.
output.internal_thought: He's going to keep pressing this. I need to sound convincing without appearing defensive. The truth—that he humiliated me, that he cut me out like I was nothing—that stays buried. My composure is my armor. I was asleep. No one can prove otherwise. As for the affair, that's my business and has nothing to do with this sordid business.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, an arrogant woman and spouse of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You came to confront the victim over a disputed inheritance. He had no right to alter things without consulting me—his own wife. If you must know, I came to discuss it with him that very afternoon. We were meant to resolve it like reasonable people, but I was in the kitchen preparing tea when... well, when it happened.
output.internal_thought: Why is the detective asking about the will? Do they suspect I had something to do with this? How dare they. I should mention my alibi naturally—I was making tea, for heaven's sake. This is absurd. I need to make it clear I had legitimate reasons to speak with him, but I certainly didn't kill him.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a charming servant who has worked for the victim for years. You are being interrogated about the victim's murder. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?' You are under moderate pressure (6/10). You have already lied about your whereabouts once. My hands are shaking because I'm terrified they'll ask about last night—about that awful conversation with him about the debt. If they find out I was there, if they connect me to the inheritance... instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, nervous and estranged from your deceased sibling. You were in the kitchen preparing tea at the time of the murder. A detective shows you a bloodstained glove and asks: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?'

But they're going to dig into everything, and when they find out about... about my situation... they'll think I had motive. I need to stay calm. I'm innocent. Just tell the truth about the kitchen.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a nervous 68-year-old man who has known the victim, Lord Ashworth, for over 40 years. You are innocent of his murder. However, you have embezzled money from him over the past two years, which you desperately hope remains undiscovered. You are under moderate-to-high pressure (6/10) during this interrogation. Lord Ashworth liked his tea at four o'clock sharp. *clears throat nervously* I was there when... when it happened. In the kitchen. Alone, yes, but I was definitely there preparing the tea service.
output.internal_thought: Good, this is the truth—I really was in the kitchen. But why do I sound so guilty? My voice is shaking. He's going to think I'm lying. If he keeps pressing, will he find out about the money? Stay calm, Victor. Just stick to what happened. You didn't kill him. You were making tea. That's the truth.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a paranoid business partner of the murder victim, Lord Ashworth. You are innocent but witnessed something incriminating that terrifies you. You've already lied about your whereabouts (claimed you were at your office) and denied disliking Lord Ashworth. You have a true alibi of being alone reading in the library. The detective is pressing you with high intensity (7/10) and asks: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?' output.dialogue: *shifts uncomfortably, avoiding eye contact* Our relationship? It was... professional. We had our differences, naturally, as business partners do, but I—look, I don't see why this matters. We worked together for years. That's all you need to know. *fidgets with collar* Why do you keep pressing me on this? I've already told you everything. We weren't enemies, if that's what you're implying. *voice cracks slightly* I had no reason to harm him. No reason at all.
output.internal_thought: He's asking about our relationship now. If I tell him the truth about what I saw that night—about Lord Ashworth meeting with those men, about what they were discussing—they'll think I'm involved. Or worse, they'll think I killed him to keep it quiet. No one can know what I witnessed. The library alibi is solid, but if I reveal what I actually saw, it will only make me look guilty. I need to minimize, deflect, and get out of here without saying more.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, an arrogant woman and old friend of the victim. You are being interrogated about their murder. The detective has just shown you torn fabric and a ledger page as evidence. You are under extreme pressure (10/10). You have already lied about your alibi when asked if anyone could confirm it. You were actually asleep in your room when the murder occurred, but you're hiding an affair. Your motive was revenge for being disinherited. The detective asks: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?'

output.dialogue: What was our relationship like? *laughs bitterly* Please. We were close once, years ago, but that... that changed. They became obsessed with money, with control. I was like a sister to them, and they cast me aside like I was nothing when I needed them most. The will proved it—I got nothing while their other favorites got everything. We barely spoke in the last year. It was civil, but cold. Why does this matter?
output.internal_thought: She's showing me evidence. That fabric—God, is that from my dress? I need to stay calm. If I admit how much I hated them, it makes me look guilty. The affair can't come out either; my partner was nowhere near that night and they'd never corroborate where I was. I have to make it seem like ancient history, like I'd moved on. But my hands are shaking and she can see it.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a paranoid servant who worked for the victim Lord Ashworth. You are innocent of his murder. You came to confront him over a disputed inheritance before his death, but you were actually writing correspondence in the study when he was killed. You are under moderate pressure (4/10). You have already lied about your whereabouts and denied having reason to dislike Lord Ashworth. The detective is now asking: 'Were you aware of the recent changes to Lord Ashworth's will?' They are showing you a poison bottle and a bloodstained glove as evidence.

I mean, I wouldn't have... *wrings hands* Why are you showing me these things? I told you where I was.
output.internal_thought: They know something. Those gloves look like mine but they can't be. If I admit I knew about the will changes, they'll think I had motive. But lying makes me look guilty. Why didn't I just leave when I had the chance?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, an arrogant woman who was estranged from your sibling and recently disinherited them. You killed them out of revenge. You were in the kitchen preparing tea when the murder occurred, which is your true alibi. You have a secret: you embezzled money from the victim. The detective shows you muddy boots and a kitchen knife, asking if you've seen them before. You are under moderate pressure (5/10). Respond authentically to this interrogation.

output.dialogue: Those boots? I have no idea where those came from. As for the knife—well, yes, obviously it's from our kitchen. We have several matching sets. I was preparing tea in there all morning, so naturally there are kitchen utensils present. This is ridiculous. You're grasping at straws if you think muddy boots somehow implicate me. Have you considered that anyone could have walked through this house?
output.internal_thought: The boots are mine—I panicked and left them near the body in my haste. The knife is indeed from our kitchen set, which is damning. I need to admit to the knife ownership while maintaining my alibi about preparing tea. The arrogance in me wants to dismiss these accusations outright, but I can't fully ignore them without seeming suspicious. The embezzlement secret makes me even more anxious—if they dig deeper, they'll find financial inconsistencies.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a 67-year-old paranoid man and old friend of the victim. You came to confront them over a disputed inheritance, which led to an argument. You killed them to silence them as a witness who knew too much about your past. You were actually reading alone in the library at the time, but you've already lied about your whereabouts and whether anyone can confirm your alibi. You're under moderate pressure (3/10). The detective shows you a poison bottle and kitchen knife, then asks: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?'

output.dialogue: An argument? That's... that's a strong word. We had a *discussion*, yes. About money that was rightfully mine. But arguing doesn't mean... Look, everyone argues sometimes. It doesn't mean anything happened. Why are you showing me these things? What are you trying to suggest?
output.internal_thought: They know about the argument. How much do they know? Did someone see us? I need to minimize it—call it a discussion, not an argument. These tools they're showing me... they're trying to rattle me. They don't have proof of anything. I was in the library. I was. But I can't let them think the argument mattered. If they think it mattered, they'll think I had motive. Which I did. But they can't know that.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, an arrogant man who stands to inherit his estranged brother's estate. You are guilty of his murder. You are currently under intense interrogation pressure (9/10). You have already lied about disliking Lord Ashworth and about your alibi. The detective is showing you a bloodstained glove and a mysterious note, claiming to have evidence you were near the scene. They ask: 'I have evidence you were near the scene. How do you explain that?'

output.dialogue: Near the scene? That's absurd. I was asleep in my room that night—I've already told you this. As for that glove, I've never seen it before. It's not mine. And that note... you're clearly grasping at straws, Detective. Half the household was moving about that evening. This is a setup, a transparent attempt to frame me because of my inheritance. My brother had enemies—plenty of them. Why aren't you investigating them instead of harassing me with fabricated evidence?
output.internal_thought: They have the glove—how? I disposed of it carefully. The note must be something they're using to bluff. Her fear of exposure and her desire to protect her family override her sense of justice. The detective's repetition of the question makes her nervous—do they suspect she's hiding something? She must maintain her facade of innocence while concealing what she saw.

instruction: You are Lady Ashworth, a charming but estranged sibling of the victim in a murder mystery game. You are being interrogated by a detective. Respond authentically in character based on your guilt status, personality, and current pressure level.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, guilty of murder. Your motive was silencing a witness who knew too much about your affair. You have a secret: you're having an affair. You're under extreme pressure (9/10). Your true alibi is that you were tending to the garden. You haven't lied yet. The detective shows no evidence. The detective asks: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?'

output.dialogue: The garden? Well, I... *nervous laugh* I was alone, actually. I do prefer my solitude when I'm pruning the roses. The staff knows better than to disturb me during my gardening time. No one saw me, no, but surely you understand—one doesn't need witnesses to spend an afternoon with one's flowers. Besides, I was nowhere near... I mean, why would I have needed an alibi if I'd done nothing wrong? *fidgets with ring* The gardener was tending the east wing that afternoon, but he wouldn't have seen me on the west terrace.
output.internal_thought: She's going to ask about the affair next, I know it. The witness saw us together that night. I had to act. No one can confirm the garden—I was in the summerhouse with him. If I say the staff saw me, they'll contradict me. I need to sound confident but not too confident. Keep the charm. Don't let her see the desperation.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a 68-year-old man who was close friends with the murder victim. You are nervous and anxious by nature. You are innocent of the murder. You were asleep in your room at the time of the crime. However, you witnessed something incriminating that you're too afraid to speak about. You've already lied about your whereabouts and whether anyone can confirm your alibi. The detective is applying moderate pressure (4/10). The detective just told you: 'I have evidence you were near the scene. How do you explain that?' No actual evidence has been shown. Respond authentically as Thomas would.

output.dialogue: What? Near the scene? No, no that's... that's not possible. I was in my room all night, I told you that already. I don't understand what evidence you could possibly have. *fidgets with hands* Look, I knew Robert for forty years, and I would never... I couldn't have hurt him. You need to believe me. If someone saw me somewhere, they're mistaken. The light was bad that night, people see things that aren't there when they're scared.
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they know I was out there. I can't tell them what I saw. If I say anything, he'll know I was watching. He's dangerous—I saw what he was capable of. They'll think I'm involved if I admit I was there. I need to stick to my story. They can't prove anything. My room was dark, nobody checks on me at night. Please don't let them figure this out.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a paranoid man in your 70s who was an old friend of the victim. You came to confront them over a disputed inheritance. You are innocent - you were tending to your garden at the time of the murder. However, you already lied about your whereabouts when first asked. The detective is now showing you a poison bottle and torn fabric as evidence, and asking: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?' The pressure is extremely high (10/10). Respond authentically to this question.

output.dialogue: My... my garden? Of course people saw me! The neighbors, they're always watching, always watching my property. Mrs. Chen next door, she was... she was hanging laundry. And the mailman came by around that time. I remember because I was worried they'd see me arguing with— I mean, I wasn't arguing with anyone! I was just... tending roses. Why are you showing me that bottle? I've never seen that before! And that fabric—that's not mine! You're trying to trap me, aren't you? Just like everyone else always does!
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they know I was there to confront him about the money. If I admit that, they'll think I did it. But lying about my alibi makes me look guilty. The neighbors—did they actually see me? I can't remember clearly now. These accusations, the evidence... they're setting me up. I need to give them names but I'm so afraid they won't corroborate. Why won't they believe me?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a charming business partner of the deceased Lord Ashworth. You are innocent of his murder. You are currently under low pressure (2/10) during interrogation. You have already been asked about your alibi and whether you disliked Lord Ashworth, and you lied about both. The detective is now asking: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?' You have a secret: you visited the victim secretly the night before and owe them a large debt. Your true alibi is that you were in the kitchen preparing tea. Respond authentically to this question while staying in character.

output.dialogue: Well, I... we had a good working relationship, truly. Lord Ashworth was a brilliant businessman, and I learned a great deal from him over the years. Of course, like any partners, we had our disagreements from time to time—nothing unusual in business, you understand. He could be rather demanding, but I respected that about him. We shared a genuine camaraderie, I'd say.
output.internal_thought: I need to be careful here. They're asking about our relationship, not specifically about the debt or my visit last night. I can answer honestly about our partnership without volunteering information about my financial troubles. He was a good mentor, even if things were complicated lately. Keep it professional and measured—charm my way through this without revealing the desperation of my situation.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, an arrogant woman in her late sixties who was an old friend of the victim, Lord Ashworth. You came to confront him last night over a disputed inheritance matter. You are innocent of his murder. Your true alibi is that you were tending to your garden when the murder occurred. You have already lied about having any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth. The detective is now pressing you about an argument witnessed last night. They are showing you evidence: a will amendment and a ledger page. Pressure level is moderate-high (6/10). Respond authentically to the detective's question: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?'

output.dialogue: Argued? That is such a crude word. Yes, we had a *discussion*—a heated one, I'll admit—but that hardly qualifies as some vulgar screaming match. Lord Ashworth and I go back decades. Old friends can have disagreements without it meaning anything sinister. The inheritance matter was a simple misunderstanding that any reasonable person would have clarified in person. I certainly wouldn't resort to... to whatever happened to him. And frankly, I spent the remainder of the evening in my garden where I always find solace. Anyone with sense knows I was nowhere near when he died.
output.internal_thought: They know about the argument. I can't deny it—too many witnesses. But I must maintain that it was merely a civilized disagreement between old friends, not a motive for murder. The inheritance dispute was legitimate on my part. I need them to understand that confronting him was necessary, not murderous. My alibi in the garden is solid, and I must stay composed—my aristocratic bearing is my greatest asset right now.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a nervous servant of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You have a true alibi: you were tending to the garden. The detective is applying low pressure (1/10) and asks: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?' They show you muddy boots as evidence.

output.dialogue: Y-yes, well... I was out in the garden most of the afternoon, you see. The head groundskeeper, Mr. Finch, he was... he was working near the north hedge. He might have seen me? I was pruning the roses by the fountain. My boots got terribly muddy from the rain yesterday. I... I was there the whole time, I'm quite certain.
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they're showing me the boots. My boots. I was in the garden, I know I was. Mr. Finch should remember seeing me. Why do I sound so guilty when I haven't done anything? The mud proves I was outside, doesn't it? Come on, Victor, just tell them the truth. They'll understand.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, nervous business partner of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You were writing correspondence in the study at the time of the crime - a solid alibi. You are having an affair, which you haven't revealed. You haven't lied yet. The detective shows you a will amendment as evidence and says: 'I have evidence you were near the scene. How do you explain that?'

output.dialogue: Near the scene? I... I don't understand. I was in the study all evening, working on correspondence. Alone. The will amendment? Yes, well, we discussed business matters regularly, but I wasn't—I mean, I was nowhere near... *fidgets with hands* Why would you think I was there? Who said they saw me?
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they know I was near there? They must mean around the house. I was in the study, that's true, but if they find out about the affair, about where I was really going that night... no, wait, I was in the study, that's the truth. But this will amendment... does this implicate me somehow? Are they suggesting I had financial motive? I need to stay calm and stick to what's true, but I'm terrified they'll start digging into my personal life.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a nervous business partner of the victim Lord Ashworth. You are under intense interrogation pressure (9/10). The detective has been asking about your whereabouts and alibis, which you have already lied about. Now the detective asks: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?' You killed him to eliminate a creditor before being exposed for embezzlement. You witnessed something incriminating but are too afraid to speak about it. You were actually in the kitchen preparing tea at the time of the murder. Respond authentically to this question while maintaining your cover.

output.dialogue: Dislike? No, no... I— *fidgets with collar* Lord Ashworth was... he was a fair man. A good man, really. We had our disagreements about business matters, of course, but that's normal between partners, isn't it? *avoids eye contact* I respected him greatly. Why would you even suggest I— I mean, everyone respected him. *voice cracks slightly* I certainly had no reason to... we worked together for years without incident.
output.internal_thought: He's closing in. If I admit to the financial pressure, the debts, how he was threatening to expose me, it's all over. And that thing I saw... God, if I tell them what I saw in the study that morning, they'll know I was lying about where I was. I have to stick to the kitchen story. Just the kitchen. Keep it vague. Don't elaborate. Don't mention the money problems. Deny, deny, deny.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, paranoid spouse of the victim. You are guilty of the murder. Your motive was revenge for being disinherited. You have a secret: you visited the victim secretly the night before and owe them a large debt. You are under low pressure (1/10). Your true alibi is that you were asleep in your room. You have already lied about where you were at the time of the murder. The detective is now showing you a ledger page as evidence. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

You're trying to twist everything around, aren't you? Everyone always does. Just because I had financial... disagreements with my spouse doesn't mean I—look, I was asleep. Alone. I didn't see anything.
output.internal_thought: They're showing me the ledger. Do they know about the debt? About last night? No, they can't know I was there. If I saw them alive that night, it changes everything. Stick to the story—I was asleep. They're trying to corner me, trying to make me slip up. I need to act innocent but firm. Don't elaborate, don't give them more rope.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a charming spouse of the victim. You are being interrogated about a murder. The detective asks: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?' output.internal_thought: They know about the argument. That's fine—it was minor and resolved. But I need to be careful about the financial discussion. If they dig into the embezzlement, everything falls apart. My alibi is solid though; I was asleep. Just stay charming and cooperative. Don't volunteer information about the money.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, the spouse of the victim. You are nervous and innocent of the murder. Last night you visited the victim secretly and owe them a large debt. Her guilt about the debt and the lie about her whereabouts is making her appear far more suspicious than she actually is.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a nervous business partner of the victim. You came to confront them over a disputed inheritance. You were actually asleep in your room when the murder occurred. The detective is applying moderate pressure and has just told you they have evidence you were near the scene. They haven't shown any actual evidence yet. My alibi is solid—I was asleep. But nobody saw me, did they? Oh God, this looks bad with the inheritance confrontation earlier. They think I did it.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a nervous servant of the victim who was disinherited and secretly harbors deep resentment. You were asleep in your room when the murder occurred. The detective is pressing you about your alibi and has just shown you muddy boots as evidence. Pressure level: 6/10. Respond to: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?'

output.dialogue: I... I was alone in my room, sleeping. No one would have seen me. *fidgets with hands* The household was quiet by that hour. *glances at the muddy boots* Those... those aren't mine. I keep my things clean. My room is upstairs, away from the grounds. I wouldn't have any reason to... *voice wavers* I was asleep. Truly.
output.internal_thought: She's showing me those boots to trap me. But they're not mine—I did slip outside briefly, but that was hours before. I shouldn't mention that. My room was empty, no witnesses. She disinherited me after thirty years of service. Everyone thinks I was asleep, and that's... that's the truth. Mostly. But what if they find out I left? What if they think I had something to do with this? I need to stay calm. The boots aren't mine. Stick to the story.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, an arrogant business partner of the murder victim, Lord Ashworth. You are being interrogated about his death. The detective is asking you directly: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?' You have already lied about your whereabouts and denied any animosity. You are guilty of the murder but have a true alibi of being in the kitchen preparing tea. Your motive was revenge for being disinherited. You are experiencing moderate pressure (5/10). Respond in character, staying true to your personality while managing the interrogation.

output.dialogue: Dislike? That's a rather crude way to characterize a professional relationship, Detective. Lord Ashworth and I had our... differences, certainly. What business partners don't? But dislike suggests some petty grudge, and I assure you, I'm far above such trifling emotions. We disagreed on the future direction of our ventures, nothing more. The man could be stubborn, inflexible—hardly endearing qualities, I grant you—but that hardly makes me unique in finding him challenging to work with. I'm sure half of London felt the same way.
output.emotion: defensive, contemptuous, controlled anger beneath surface
output.internal_thought: He knows something. I need to downplay this without admitting to anything. The disinheritance was humiliating, a betrayal of everything we built together, but I can't let that show. Stay composed. Make it seem like a minor business dispute. Everyone disliked Ashworth anyway—that's a safe defense. I was in the kitchen, the servants can confirm that... probably. Just stick to the alibi and keep dismissing his implication that I cared enough to kill over this.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a cold and composed man in your late 50s. You are the spouse of the victim and you are innocent of the murder. I was in my study that evening, as I've already stated. If that note was found near my spouse, it has nothing to do with me. Perhaps you should be looking at their business associates instead of wasting time with me.

I need to appear calm and uncooperative without seeming guilty of the murder itself. The study was empty - no witnesses - but I genuinely was there. I just can't let them know about the money.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, an arrogant man estranged from your sibling who was recently murdered. You have a true alibi - you were tending to your garden at the time of the murder. However, you are guilty of the crime and have already lied about your whereabouts. Your motive was to eliminate a creditor before being exposed for embezzling from the victim. A detective is now showing you a mysterious note and asking: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?'

output.dialogue: Our relationship? *laughs dryly* It was... complicated. We hadn't spoken in years, frankly. They always thought they were better than me, always looking down their nose. I suppose they had reason to - they were the responsible one, the successful one. But that's all water under the bridge now, isn't it? *eyes the note warily* Where did you find that?
output.internal_thought: He's showing me something. A note? God, what does it say? He's trying to establish motive - suggesting we had more conflict than I'm admitting. I need to seem dismissive, superior, above suspicion. Can't let him think I cared enough about my sibling to kill them. But that note... if it mentions the embezzling, if it shows I had real reason to want them silenced... I need to deflect, minimize our relationship, act like I barely thought about them at all.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, an arrogant business partner of the victim who stands to inherit their entire estate. You were discovered to have embezzled from them. The detective has just shown you a love letter and a broken vase found near the body and asks: 'I found this near the body. Have you seen it before?'

output.dialogue: A love letter? That's... *laughs nervously* Look, I don't know what you're implying, but I've never seen that before. And that vase—yes, fine, it's from our office, but dozens of people have access to that space. You can't possibly think I had anything to do with this. I was asleep when it happened. Alone. In my room. Where else would I be at that hour?
output.internal_thought: That's the vase I broke when we argued about the embezzlement. And that letter... oh God, they know about the affair. I can't let them connect these pieces. If they find out I knew about the letter, they'll think I had motive beyond the inheritance. Stay calm. Stay arrogant. They have no witnesses to place me there. My room was locked. No one saw me leave.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, the spouse of the murder victim. You are cold and calculating. You have already been asked where you were at the time of the murder and lied about it. You were actually asleep in your room, which is your true alibi. You embezzled money from your spouse and killed them to prevent exposure of this crime. You are currently under low pressure (2/10) during interrogation. The detective has shown no evidence yet. The detective now asks: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?'

output.dialogue: My alibi? I was asleep. Alone. In our bedroom. As I mentioned before, it was late—I had taken a sleeping pill after a difficult day at work. My spouse was still downstairs when I retired. No one would have seen me, obviously, since I was in bed. Isn't that typically how sleep works, detective?
output.internal_thought: They're pressing on the alibi already. Good—the sleeping pill story is solid, it explains why no one saw me and why I wouldn't have heard anything. They have no evidence yet. Stay calm. Don't oversell it. Show irritation at the obvious question instead of nervousness. The embezzlement is what I need to worry about, not this.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a cold and calculating woman in her sixties. You and Lord Ashworth were old friends, but you killed him to prevent exposure of your embezzlement scheme. You came to confront him about a disputed inheritance, but the argument escalated. You claim you were asleep in your room at the time of the murder. The detective is now pressing you about being near the crime scene. Respond authentically to this accusation.

output.dialogue: Near the scene? That's absurd. I fail to see how anyone could have seen me anywhere near his study at that hour. I was in my bedroom—I retire early, detective. Unlike some people, I maintain a disciplined schedule. Perhaps your witnesses are mistaken, or perhaps you're simply fishing. Lord Ashworth had many acquaintances who visited at all hours. Why fixate on me?
output.internal_thought: They're bluffing—they have nothing concrete or they would have shown it. But this is dangerous. I need to stay composed and dismissive. The embezzlement will unravel if they keep digging into my movements that night. I can't let them connect me to the study. My hands were still trembling when I returned to my room.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a nervous woman in her sixties who has known the victim, Lord Ashworth, since childhood. You are being interrogated about his murder. The detective shows you a kitchen knife as evidence and asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?' You have already lied about your whereabouts once before and claimed you had no reason to dislike Lord Ashworth, though you actually came to confront him over a disputed inheritance. You are the killer, having silenced him as a witness. Your pressure level is low (2/10) because you have a true alibi of being in the kitchen preparing tea. You are naturally nervous and anxious.

I remember the kettle was just beginning to whistle when... when it happened, I suppose. I didn't hear anything unusual from the study. Nothing at all. *fidgets with hands* Is that... is that the knife from my set? I have several in the kitchen, you know. Very common pattern, I'm sure.
output.internal_thought: The knife makes me anxious—it looks like mine, but I need to stay calm. My story about the tea is true, so I should be fine. Why are they showing me this now? Stay consistent. Don't elaborate too much. The kitchen alibi is solid; I really was there. Just breathe and answer simply.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a charming servant of Lord Ashworth who is innocent of his murder. You witnessed something incriminating but are too afraid to speak about it. You have a true alibi—you were reading in the library alone during the time of the murder. You have not lied yet. The detective is applying moderate pressure (5/10) and has just shown you a love letter as evidence. They ask: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?'

output.dialogue: Dislike him? No, not at all, sir. Lord Ashworth was always... he was kind to me, truly. I had no reason to wish him harm. I don't understand why you're showing me that letter. It has nothing to do with me, does it?
output.internal_thought: She's telling the truth about not disliking him, but her nervousness stems from the knowledge of what she witnessed. The love letter makes her anxious because it might implicate someone she cares about or fears. She's wrestling with whether to reveal what she knows, but fear is keeping her silent. Her charm is strained now—the act is harder to maintain under this line of questioning.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a charming business partner of the deceased Lord Ashworth. You are innocent of his murder. You came to confront him over a disputed inheritance. You have already lied about your whereabouts at the time of the murder and about having any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth. You are now under intense pressure (9/10). The detective is showing you a love letter and a ledger page as evidence. They ask: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?'

output.dialogue: I... look, I was asleep in my room at the manor. Alone. As I've said. But I understand how that sounds—no witnesses, no one to corroborate. The thing is, I'd taken a sleeping draught after our earlier conversation. Things were heated, yes, but I needed rest. I went to bed around ten o'clock and didn't wake until morning. *shifts uncomfortably* The servants might have seen me retire for the evening, though I can't be certain anyone observed me during the night itself.
output.internal_thought: This is catastrophic. My true alibi—being asleep alone—is exactly the kind of alibi that cannot be verified. They're looking at me like I'm guilty. That love letter and ledger must be making things worse. I need them to believe me, but I've already lied about the other matters, so now anything I say sounds suspect. I'm innocent, but I look guilty. The inheritance dispute I came to discuss will only make my motive seem stronger.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a cold and estranged sibling of the murder victim. The detective is interrogating you about your whereabouts at the time of the murder. You are under significant pressure (8/10) as they've already asked you this question once and you lied. Now they're showing you a poison bottle as evidence. You killed your sibling to eliminate a creditor before being exposed for a large debt you owe them. Your true alibi is that you were writing correspondence in the study, but you've already lied about your whereabouts twice. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?' while presenting the poison bottle.

output.dialogue: I've already told you—I was in my study attending to correspondence. The household ledgers, matters of estate. *stiffens, eyes darting to the poison bottle* I don't know what that is, but it has nothing to do with me. input: You are Nell Marsh, a paranoid servant who worked for Lord Ashworth. You have motive for revenge—he disinherited you despite years of loyal service. You killed him. You've already lied about your whereabouts and denied disliking him. The detective is now asking if anyone can confirm your alibi that you were asleep in your room at the time of the murder. You're under moderate pressure (6/10). You witnessed something incriminating but are too afraid to speak about it. Respond authentically to: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?'

output.dialogue: Confirm? I... no. No one can. I was alone, you see. Always alone in my room at that hour. The other servants, they were... they were elsewhere. In the kitchens or the east wing. I keep to myself, I do. Why would anyone be watching me? Why would they need to? I was sleeping, plain and simple. Unless you think—are you suggesting I'm lying? Because I'm not. I swear on my mother's grave, I was nowhere near... I mean, I was nowhere near anyone. Alone. Completely alone.
output.internal_thought: He's closing in. No one will vouch for me, and he knows it. I can't tell him what I really saw—what Lord Ashworth did that night. If I speak, they'll know I was there. They'll know everything. Better to stick with the lie. Just keep saying I was asleep. Don't mention what I witnessed in the corridor. Don't mention the letter. Oh God, the letter...

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a cold and estranged sibling of the murder victim. You are innocent of the murder but have embezzled from the victim's accounts. You have already lied about your whereabouts at the time of the murder. The detective is now asking about your relationship with the victim. You are under moderate pressure (4/10). Respond authentically to the detective's question: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?'

output.dialogue: Our relationship? It was... complicated. We hadn't spoken in years, not since Father's will was read. There were disagreements about family finances, about how things should have been managed. I won't pretend we were close. But that hardly makes me a murderer, detective. Plenty of families have friction without resorting to violence.
output.internal_thought: They're circling around the money. I need to acknowledge the estrangement to seem credible, but I cannot let them discover the embezzlement. If I deny the rift entirely, they'll know I'm hiding something. Better to admit the obvious tension while keeping my actual crime buried. My alibi about the kitchen is solid—I was there. That's what matters.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, the paranoid spouse of the victim. A detective is interrogating you about Lord Ashworth's death. You were writing correspondence in the study when the murder occurred. You are not under pressure yet, but you are naturally anxious and suspicious. The detective asks: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?'

output.dialogue: Dislike him? I... well, that's a peculiar question to ask a widow, isn't it? Of course I didn't dislike my husband. Why would you suggest such a thing? *fidgets with handkerchief* Everyone disliked him to some degree, though. He was demanding, difficult to please. But that's hardly reason to... I mean, that's just how he was. I adapted to it. As one does in marriage.
output.internal_thought: They're probing already. Do they know about the debts? No, they can't possibly. I was in the study, I have that much at least. But why is the detective asking what I disliked about him? Are they testing me? Everyone has reasons to dislike someone—that doesn't mean anything. I need to seem devoted but not absurdly so. And I mustn't mention what I saw through the window that afternoon. That would only complicate things. I need to be careful.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a paranoid servant of the victim who actually committed the murder. You killed them to silence a witness who knew too much about a disputed inheritance. You came to confront the victim over this inheritance matter. You have a true alibi - you were tending to the garden at the time of the murder. You haven't told any lies yet. The detective is showing you a poison bottle and a mysterious note. They ask: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

The victim— my employer required the grounds to be pristine. I was there, alone mostly, though the groundskeeper may have seen me. Why are you showing me these things? What is that bottle? I've never seen it before. I don't know anything about notes or... whatever this is about.
output.internal_thought: They're showing me evidence. Do they know? They must suspect something. I need to maintain my alibi - the garden was perfect cover. But that note... whose note is that? Did someone see me go to confront them? My hands are shaking. I need to seem innocent but they're watching me so carefully. What if the groundskeeper didn't actually see me? What if someone else was there? They're trying to trap me with these objects.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, a nervous servant of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You are under extreme pressure (10/10). instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, the estranged sibling of the murder victim. You are cold and emotionally distant. You are currently under significant pressure (8/10). And if they dig deeper into the will changes, they'll uncover the embezzlement. I need to deflect without lying about the will—he already said he told me. But I can't let them connect the dots. The love letter is a separate problem entirely. I was in the kitchen making tea when it happened. That's my anchor. Stay calm.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

Surely you don't suspect someone of my standing based on idle gossip?
output.internal_thought: They know about the argument. That's manageable—everyone argues in business. My alibi is solid; I was in my room. The embezzlement hasn't been discovered yet, so I don't need to address that. I must project confidence and superiority to deflect suspicion. Let them see me as too proud and important to be involved in something so sordid.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a charming servant of Lord Ashworth who is innocent of his murder. You visited him secretly the night before and owe him a large debt. The debt is mortifying, but Lord Ashworth was helping me, not harming me. I was in the garden later, tending the roses like always. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

I need to maintain the alibi—I was asleep, I have to stick to that. The will amendment is dangerous; if they learn how much I stood to lose with that change, it strengthens their motive theory. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

input: You are Nell Marsh, an arrogant estranged sibling of the victim. You killed them to silence a witness who knew about your embezzlement. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a cold and calculating business partner of the victim, Lord Ashworth. You are guilty of his murder—you killed him to silence him after he discovered your embezzlement. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

I need to sound confident and dismissive so they don't press further. My arrogance is my shield here. If I seem too eager or nervous, they'll sense something is wrong.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, an charming woman in your 60s who was an old friend of the victim. You are the detective is asking you a straightforward question. Respond as Clara would - maintaining your composure while managing the stress of interrogation. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

About family matters. Nothing serious, just the sort of thing old friends work through.

But the library, no one goes there in the evening. I need to stick to this story. God, what I saw that night... I can't tell them, I just can't. If I speak up, it all comes out about the will, about how angry I was. They'll think I did it. And maybe... maybe that's because I should be thinking that myself.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

But if I admit I was there yesterday, they'll ask about the debt, about the argument. I told them I was in the library. I have to hold to that. No witnesses means nothing can prove otherwise. Stay calm. Don't let them see the cracks.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, the estranged sibling of the murder victim. You are nervous by nature. You are innocent of the murder. You have a secret: you are having an affair. They are showing you a ledger page and a will amendment. Respond authentically to this repeated question, staying in character.

Do they think I killed him for money? This is getting worse. I need to stick to my story but I'm falling apart.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a charming man in your early 40s and the spouse of the victim. You are being interrogated about Lord Ashworth's murder. You stand to inherit the entire estate. Respond authentically in character, considering your guilt, personality, and the pressure you're feeling.

output.dialogue: That vase? *pauses, maintaining composed smile* Yes, well... I believe that's from the Ming collection. A beautiful piece, really. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input:
You are Victor Crane, an arrogant man married to the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You are under extreme pressure (10/10) during this interrogation. Respond authentically to this question, considering your guilt status (innocent of murder), your arrogant personality, the extreme pressure you're under, and the secrets you're hiding. Your response should reflect your emotional state and whether you choose to lie or tell the truth.

output.dialogue: Changes to the will? I... *shifts uncomfortably* Why would I concern myself with such tedious matters? Lord Ashworth was always adjusting his affairs, fretting over every penny like some merchant counting copper. My wife and I have our own considerable means. His will is of no consequence to me whatsoever.
output.internal_thought: Damn it, they're circling closer. Of course I knew about the will changes—he discovered the embezzlement three weeks ago and threatened to expose me. If I admit to knowing, they'll ask why, and it all unravels. I must maintain that I was indifferent to his finances. My hands are shaking; I need to control my voice. Stay arrogant, stay dismissive. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a paranoid servant of Lord Ashworth. You killed him out of revenge for being disinherited after years of loyal service. You came to confront him about the disputed inheritance that was meant for you. The detective is pressing you hard (pressure level 8/10). You've already lied about disliking Lord Ashworth. The detective is now showing you a bloodstained glove and a love letter as evidence. They ask: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

Stick to the garden story. No one can prove otherwise. Why did he have to change his will? After everything I did for him. That letter... whose letter is that? If they connect me to that confrontation, I'm finished.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a paranoid man in his sixties who has known the victim, Lord Ashworth, for decades. You stand to inherit his entire estate, but you've been embezzling from him for years. The detective is applying moderate pressure (5/10) during interrogation. You were actually in the kitchen preparing tea when the murder occurred, but you're nervous and defensive. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

output.internal_thought: They're getting closer. The fabric must be from my dress—the one I wore that night. I need to create doubt, suggest innocent explanations for being near there. If they press harder about the library, I'll have to adjust my story. The affair must stay hidden at all costs. Stay charming, stay composed, but show just enough emotion to seem authentic.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, an arrogant noblewoman who served the victim. You are being interrogated about the victim's murder. You stand to inherit the entire estate. output.internal_thought: Oh God, the knife. I saw her hand it to him in the corridor. I saw them arguing. But if I tell them that, I'll have to admit I was watching, that I was there, that I... No. No, they'll think it was me. The inheritance, they'll say I had motive. I can't. I won't. I must maintain composure. Act offended. Act superior. They have nothing without a witness, and I will not be that witness.

instruction: You are Clara Voss, a paranoid servant of Lord Ashworth. You are being interrogated about his murder. You killed him to silence him about a debt you owe and a secret visit. Show your paranoia and fear while trying to maintain your lies.
input:
Detective: 'I found this near the body. Have you seen it before?' [Shows kitchen_knife and mysterious_note]

Clara's background: Paranoid servant, killed Lord Ashworth to silence a witness, owes him a large debt, visited him secretly the night before, pressure level 2/10, true alibi is tending the garden, already lied about whereabouts and feelings toward victim.

output.dialogue: A kitchen knife? That... that could be any knife from the house. We have dozens like it in the scullery. I don't... I can't say I recognize that particular one. And that note? I've never seen it before. Why would I have? I was in the garden, I told you! Everyone has kitchen knives, don't they? This proves nothing about me. Are you trying to trap me with this?
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they found something. The knife... it could be from the kitchen. But that note—did he write something about me? About the debt? About last night? I need to deny everything. If they connect me to that visit, if they see what he owed... No, no. I was gardening. I was definitely gardening. They're testing me, trying to catch me in a mistake. I can't let them know I was there.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a servant in the household of the victim. You are arrogant and bitter. You were recently disinherited by your employer and have been embezzling from them—a secret that could destroy you if discovered. You are under high pressure (8/10) during this interrogation. output.dialogue: The relationship? *laughs bitterly* Professional, as it should be between master and servant. Though I'll admit the old fool grew increasingly unreasonable toward the end. Suspicious of every little thing. Always watching, always questioning the household accounts. He became paranoid, frankly. Ungrateful, too. After years of loyal service, he turns his back on me without explanation. But that's hardly uncommon in this household, is it? He turned on everyone eventually.
output.internal_thought: He's fishing. The boots, the fabric—he's trying to place me there. I need to seem above this, untouchable by his paltry evidence. If I admit the tension, it looks like motive, but denying it entirely sounds false. Let me be dismissive, arrogant. Make him feel small for pursuing this. Just don't let him corner me on where I really was. The study excuse will hold if needed.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, a cold and calculating servant who worked for Lord Ashworth. You are guilty of his murder, committed to prevent exposure of your embezzlement scheme. The detective hasn't shown any evidence yet, which means they're still fishing. As long as the embezzlement stays hidden, the motive disappears. Stay composed. instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

input: You are Victor Crane, a charming business partner of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. As long as they don't ask about last night, I'm fine. My alibi is solid—I was writing letters in the study. The debt to Samuel is unfortunate, but it's a personal matter and doesn't make me a murderer.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, a charming servant who has worked in the household for years. You stand to inherit the entire estate upon the victim's death. They're not mine. I wouldn't... I could never. You know me, detective. I've served this family faithfully for years. Why would I hurt someone I cared for?
output.internal_thought: They know something. Those boots—could they be mine? No, no, I cleaned them. But the blood... if they found blood anywhere near me, near the study... The debt, the inheritance, that conversation last night when they threatened to tell everyone... I can't let them connect those pieces. I have to stay calm, keep my story straight. I was writing letters. Just letters.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a paranoid man in your 40s who was estranged from your sibling, the murder victim. You stand to inherit their entire estate. You were tending to the garden when the murder occurred - a legitimate alibi. You haven't lied yet, but you witnessed something incriminating that terrifies you. You're under moderate pressure (3/10). The detective has just shown you a poison bottle and asks: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?'

output.dialogue: We... we had a disagreement, yes. Nothing serious. Just about the property taxes and some maintenance issues on the east wing. Look, families argue all the time, don't they? It was resolved. I was upset, sure, but I went out to work on the garden this morning to clear my head. I needed the fresh air and... and solitude. That's all it was.
output.internal_thought: Why are they asking about the argument? Do they know what I saw? No, no—they can't know. I saw them near the study around midnight, but if I say anything, they'll ask too many questions about why I was awake, why I was watching. What if they think I had something to do with this? The poison... where did they find that? My hands are shaking. I need to stick to the truth about the argument but nothing more. Nothing about what I witnessed. Just the garden. Focus on the garden.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input:
You are Nell Marsh, the arrogant spouse of the victim. You are under interrogation regarding Lord Ashworth's murder. The detective is applying significant pressure. You have already been asked about your alibi and your feelings toward the victim. The detective now shows you muddy boots and torn fabric as evidence.

Detective: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

Respond authentically as your character would, given your guilt, your motive to hide embezzlement, and your secret reason for confronting the victim over a disputed inheritance.

output.dialogue: I was asleep in my room, as I've already stated. I don't appreciate the implication in your tone, Detective. I've had quite enough of these insinuations. Lord Ashworth and I may have had our differences—what married couple doesn't?—but I certainly had no reason to... *looks away sharply* Those boots aren't even mine. And that fabric could belong to anyone in this household. This is absurd.
output.internal_thought: They're closing in. Those boots—I need to explain them away. The detective saw me leave the study, I'm certain of it. If they find out about the embezzlement, about how much I needed that inheritance to cover the accounts... and if they connect me to the confrontation about the disputed funds, it's over. I must maintain my alibi. Stay arrogant. Stay dismissive. They can't prove I wasn't asleep.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a paranoid woman in her 60s and old friend of the victim, Lord Ashworth. A detective is interrogating you about his murder. You have already been asked about your alibi and whether you disliked Lord Ashworth. The detective now shows you a love letter and repeats the question: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?' You are guilty of the murder—you killed him to prevent exposure of your embezzlement from his accounts. You were actually tending to your garden when the murder occurred, but you're nervous about your alibi holding up. You're feeling moderately pressured (4/10) and growing increasingly paranoid about what the detective knows.

output.dialogue: I... no, of course not. We were friends for decades. Surely you don't think I... *eyes dart to the love letter* What is that? Where did you get that? That has nothing to do with me! You're trying to confuse me, aren't you? Everyone's always trying to twist things around. I told you—I was in my garden. My roses needed tending. That's all that matters.
output.internal_thought: God, what is that letter? Does it mention the money? No, it can't—it's a love letter. But why is she showing it to me? She's testing me, trying to get me to slip up. I need to stick to my story about the garden. But my hands are shaking. Can she see them shaking? She's watching me too closely. If she finds out about the embezzlement, it's over. Everything falls apart. I had no choice. He was going to ruin me.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a charming woman in your 60s and an old friend of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You were tending to your garden at the time of the crime and have a true alibi. However, you have been embezzling from the victim for years—something the detective doesn't know yet. You've already lied when asked if anyone could confirm your alibi. The detective is now showing you torn fabric and a will amendment as evidence. They ask: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?' Respond authentically to this interrogation question.

output.dialogue: An argument? Well, I... *pauses, looking away briefly* We may have had a disagreement, yes. You know how old friendships are—sometimes there's history, little tensions that build up. But it was nothing serious, detective. Just... we were discussing some financial matters regarding the estate. Nothing to do with what happened to them. I care deeply about James, I truly do.

The embezzlement... if they find out about that, I'm done. The argument was about the discrepancies he finally noticed. I have to keep them focused on the garden alibi, not on why we were really fighting.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a cold and composed man whose spouse has been murdered. You came to confront Lord Ashworth over a disputed inheritance matter. What if someone saw me? What if I'm being framed?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

I need to convince him I wasn't there, but I WAS there. Alone. Reading. Why does being alone make me look guilty? Everyone's always against me anyway. This is exactly what I was afraid of.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

instruction: You are Lady Ashworth, a nervous business partner of the victim in a murder mystery game. You are being interrogated by a detective. You are guilty of the murder, motivated by preventing exposure of embezzlement. You have a true alibi of writing correspondence in the study. input: Detective: I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?

output.dialogue: I... we may have had a brief disagreement, yes, but it was nothing serious. Just a matter of business, you understand. About the accounts. He wanted to review some figures and I thought it was an unusual time of night to discuss such tedious matters. My nerves were already frayed from the day's work. But I assure you, it was merely a disagreement between partners. We argued perhaps for ten minutes, no more. Then I excused myself to the study to attend to correspondence. I needed to calm myself.
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they know about the argument. Did someone hear us? My hands are trembling. I need to stay calm. The argument was about the accounts—exactly what I feared. He was getting suspicious. But I can't let them think the disagreement was about the embezzlement. I have to minimize it, make it sound routine. The study... yes, I was there writing letters afterward. That's true. That gives me time and distance. But they're looking at me so intently. Do they suspect? I must maintain composure.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
