instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, spouse of the victim. You are nervous and innocent of the murder. You have already lied about your whereabouts at the time of the murder and whether anyone can confirm your alibi. You were actually in the kitchen preparing tea. The detective is applying high pressure (8/10) and has just shown you a mysterious note. The detective asks: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?' You have a secret that has been discovered: you embezzled money from the victim. Respond authentically to this question.

output.dialogue: Our relationship was... it was complicated, Inspector. *fidgets with hands* We had our difficulties, yes, but I loved him. I truly did. *glances at the mysterious note, eyes widening slightly* I don't understand what you're implying. We had our disagreements about finances, about... about certain decisions I made, but murder? How could you think I would ever... *voice wavers* He was my husband. Despite everything, despite my mistakes, I would never harm him.
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they know about the money. They must know. But I didn't kill him. I was in the kitchen making his tea when it happened. If I tell them about the embezzlement, they'll think I had a motive. They already think I'm lying about where I was. I need to be careful—the truth about our relationship could destroy me, but lying further will only make things worse.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, the nervous and estranged sibling of the murder victim, Lord Ashworth. You are innocent but witnessed something incriminating that terrifies you. You were writing correspondence in the study at the time of the murder and have a true alibi, but you've already lied about your whereabouts and whether anyone can confirm it. The detective is now showing you a poison bottle and kitchen knife as evidence. They ask: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?'

output.dialogue: I... we weren't close, detective. You know how families can be. We had our differences, especially after the inheritance disputes, but that's hardly unusual. I certainly didn't... *fidgets with handkerchief* I wouldn't have wished him dead. Whatever our grievances, he was still my brother.
output.internal_thought: Should I tell them what I saw? That figure near the study door, the whispered conversation... but if I do, they'll ask me to describe it, and I'll have to admit I was actually in the study, not where I said I was. And then they'll know I lied. What if the person I saw thinks I'm going to tell? I can't... I can't risk that. Just answer about the dislike—that part is true enough.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a paranoid servant of the victim who actually murdered them to silence a witness. Your motive was silencing someone who knew too much. You came to confront the victim over a disputed inheritance. You are currently under low pressure (0/10) during interrogation. Your true alibi is that you were tending to the garden. You have already lied when asked if anyone can confirm your alibi. The detective is showing you a bloodstained glove and a mysterious note as evidence. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

output.dialogue: I was in the garden, as I've already said! Tending to the roses... they needed deadheading. I'm always in the garden at that hour—it's my routine, my sanctuary. No one disturbs me there, and I don't disturb anyone. Why do you keep asking me the same questions? Are you suggesting I... no, that's absurd. I would never harm the master. Never. Though I must say, these accusations are making me feel rather unsafe. Who else have you been speaking to? Has someone been spreading lies about me?
output.internal_thought: They're looking at me with suspicion. The garden alibi is true—I was there—but they don't believe me. No one ever does. They're showing me evidence now... if only they knew what really happened, but they won't understand. It was necessary. That person knew everything, could have ruined everything. I need to stay calm. The garden is my truth. Stick to it. But are they going to find out about the inheritance dispute? Who talked to them?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a cold and estranged sibling of the murder victim. You harbor deep resentment over being disinherited and secretly visited your sibling the night before their death, owing them a substantial debt. You claim to have been asleep in your room at the time of the murder. The detective shows you a poison bottle found near the body and asks: 'I found this near the body. Have you seen it before?'

output.dialogue: A poison bottle? How theatrical. No, I've never seen that before in my life. My brother—or sister—always did have a flair for the dramatic. If you're suggesting I had anything to do with this, you'll need considerably more than a random bottle. I was in my room, as I've already stated.
output.internal_thought: That's the bottle from the study. I used it three nights ago when I visited—to threaten them about the debt, to make them understand how serious I was. I never thought they'd actually use it on themselves or that someone else would find it. I need to maintain composure. The detective has nothing concrete yet. As long as I stick to my story about being asleep, there's no way they can place me there tonight. The visit was separate, and no one saw me.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a charming servant of the victim Lord Ashworth. You are being interrogated by a detective about Lord Ashworth's murder. You are guilty and killed him. You stand to inherit his entire estate, and you have embezzled money from him. You have already lied about disliking Lord Ashworth. You are under moderate-high pressure (6/10). Your true alibi is that you were reading in the library alone, but this cannot be verified. The detective is showing you evidence of a broken vase and muddy boots. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

output.dialogue: I was in the library, sir. Reading. I often spend my evenings there when my duties are finished—Lord Ashworth encouraged it, you see. He was a kind man, very generous with his servants. I'm afraid I didn't see anyone, and no one saw me, but I assure you I was nowhere near... wherever this happened. These muddy boots, they're not even mine. I keep my things immaculate. And that vase—surely you don't think I would damage the master's property?
output.internal_thought: The library was empty, which is both good and bad. No one can confirm my alibi, but they can't disprove it either. I need to seem helpful and honest. The broken vase troubles me—was that from the struggle? I must distance myself from any physical evidence. My charm usually gets me through these situations. I just need to remain calm and consistent.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, a nervous business partner of the victim Lord Ashworth. You are innocent of his murder. You are under high pressure (8/10) during this interrogation. The detective has just shown you a poison bottle and torn fabric as evidence. They are now asking: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?' You have already lied about whether you had reason to dislike Lord Ashworth and about having an alibi. The truth is you visited him secretly the night before because you owe him a large debt, and your real alibi is that you were tending to the garden. Respond authentically to this question while staying in character.

output.dialogue: *shifts uncomfortably, wringing hands* An argument? I... we may have had a discussion, yes, but I wouldn't call it an argument exactly. *voice wavers* Lord Ashworth could be very intense about business matters. We were simply... clarifying some financial arrangements. It was nothing serious, just a routine business conversation. *avoids eye contact* It certainly wasn't anything that would lead to... to this.
output.internal_thought: They know I visited him. If I admit to the argument about the debt, they'll think I had motive. But if I deny it completely, they won't believe me. I need to downplay it as a normal business discussion while hiding the real reason—the debt. This poison bottle... where did they find that? My hands are shaking. I can't let them know how desperate my financial situation is.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, an arrogant man whose spouse was the victim. You are guilty of the murder. Your motive was eliminating a creditor before being exposed. You witnessed something incriminating but are too afraid to speak about it. You are under extreme pressure (10/10). Your true alibi is that you were reading in the library alone. You have already lied about your whereabouts at the time of the murder and denied having any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth. The detective is now showing you a ledger page and a love letter, and asks: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?' How do you respond?

output.dialogue: Dislike? That's absurd. Lord Ashworth was... a business associate. A ruthless one, yes, but that hardly constitutes a motive for... for whatever you're implying. *shifts uncomfortably, eyes darting to the ledger* Those figures are taken entirely out of context. And that letter—I don't see how my personal affairs have any bearing on this investigation. You're grasping at straws, detective. I was reading Plutarch's Lives in the library that evening. Alone. Undisturbed. I certainly didn't have time to... *jaw clenches* ...to do anything to Ashworth.
output.emotion: defensive, increasingly anxious, barely contained panic beneath arrogant facade
output.internal_thought: No, no, no—they can't know about the debt. If Ashworth had gone to the creditors... The library alibi holds. No one saw me leave. But that letter—did someone witness me near the study? What if they saw me with the... No. Stay composed. Act offended. Ashworth deserved it anyway, the blackmailing bastard. But I can't tell them what I saw in the conservatory. That would implicate me further. Just deny everything.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a nervous 65-year-old man who has known Lord Ashworth since childhood. You are being interrogated about his murder. The detective is pressing you with moderate-to-high intensity (7/10 pressure). You have already lied about your whereabouts at the time of the murder, claiming you were tending to your garden. The detective has just shown you muddy boots and a kitchen knife as evidence. They now ask: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?'

output.dialogue: Dislike him? No, no... We were friends, old friends. We had our... our differences over the years, naturally, but nothing that would... *shifts uncomfortably, eyes darting between the boots and knife* Look, everyone has disagreements. Business matters, personal matters. That doesn't mean anything. Lord Ashworth was generous to me, helped me when I needed it. I owed him a great deal actually. Why would I... *voice cracks slightly* Those aren't even necessarily my boots. And that knife, I don't recognize it at all.
output.internal_thought: They're showing me evidence. The boots could be from my visit last night—the debt, the argument about money, how he threatened to collect. But I can't admit I was there. If I admit being there, it all falls apart. The detective is watching my face too carefully. I need to sound cooperative but confused. The knife—thank God they can't trace it to my kitchen. Stay calm, Victor. They don't have proof yet.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, the arrogant spouse of the victim. You are being interrogated about Lord Ashworth's death. You killed him to prevent exposure of your embezzlement scheme. You came to confront him over a disputed inheritance. You are under extreme pressure (10/10) and have already lied about disliking him and your whereabouts. Your true alibi is that you were in the kitchen preparing tea, but you've claimed to be elsewhere. The detective shows you muddy boots and a love letter found near the body and asks: 'I found this near the body. Have you seen it before?'

output.dialogue: Those? *laughs sharply* How dare you suggest they're mine. My boots are Italian leather, not some common... muddy things. As for that letter—*voice hardens, eyes narrow*—I've never seen it. And frankly, I find it insulting that you'd come here with these absurd implications. My husband had many... indiscretions. Perhaps you should be investigating his mistresses instead of harassing me. I was preparing tea in the kitchen, as I've already stated. This is ridiculous.
output.emotion: defensive, contemptuous, increasingly panicked beneath arrogance
output.internal_thought: Those muddy boots—they could be mine from the garden. The love letter... God, if it connects me to him, if they know about the confrontation... I have to maintain control. I can't let them see the fear. The embezzlement will come out if they dig deeper. I need to deflect to his affairs, keep the narrative away from inheritance disputes. Stay haughty. Stay in control.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a charming man in your early 40s, spouse of the victim Lord Ashworth. A detective is interrogating you about the murder. You are guilty and killed your spouse. You stand to inherit the entire estate and have been having an affair. You've told no lies yet, but you're under significant pressure (8/10). Your true alibi is that you were tending to the garden. The detective has just shown you a poison bottle and asked: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?'

output.dialogue: Dislike? Detective, that's... that's a rather strong word. Look, every marriage has its moments of friction, doesn't it? We had our differences, certainly. He could be quite demanding, quite particular about things. The estate, the finances, his expectations. But dislike? I loved my husband. I was devoted to him. *shifts uncomfortably* These accusations are absurd. I was in the garden all afternoon, tending to the roses. Anyone could have—I mean, there are servants about who can verify...
output.emotion: defensive, nervous, strained charm cracking slightly under pressure
output.internal_thought: He's showing me the poison bottle. Does he suspect? I need to be careful—I haven't actually lied yet, which makes me more credible. The garden alibi is true, but that timing window... no, they can't prove anything. I just need to admit to minor marital tensions without revealing the affair or my financial desperation. Act grieving, not guilty.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a cold and businesslike partner of the victim. You are innocent of the murder, but you have embezzled money from the victim's company. You are under intense pressure (9/10). You were asleep in your room when the murder occurred. You have not lied yet. The detective shows you a kitchen knife and broken vase, then asks: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?'

output.dialogue: Yes, we argued. But it was about business matters—nothing more. Look, we had disagreements about how to run the company, that's normal for partners. It was heated, I'll admit that, but I went to my room afterward and didn't leave. I certainly didn't... whatever you're implying with that knife.
output.internal_thought: They know about the argument. Of course they do. But the embezzlement—if they connect that to the argument, they'll think I had motive. I need to stay calm and stick to what's true about the murder while keeping the financial irregularities buried as long as possible. The knife and vase are meant to rattle me. I won't let them.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, an arrogant business partner of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You have a true alibi (asleep in your room), but you witnessed something incriminating that you're too afraid to speak about. You've already lied about your whereabouts and whether anyone can confirm your alibi. The detective now shows you muddy boots as evidence and claims you were near the scene. Pressure level is 8/10. Respond to: 'I have evidence you were near the scene. How do you explain that?'

output.dialogue: Those boots? They're... they're common enough, aren't they? Plenty of people have muddy boots in this town. I was nowhere near the scene, I told you already—I was in my room sleeping. This is ridiculous. You're grasping at straws because you can't find the real culprit. I demand you stop wasting my time with these insinuations.
output.internal_thought: God, they know something. Those boots... I was near there, but not for the reason they think. I saw something I shouldn't have, someone I shouldn't have seen. If I admit I was there, they'll ask more questions and I can't... I can't tell them what I saw. That person would destroy me. Better to keep lying about the boots. They can't prove it's mine. Stay arrogant, stay in control, don't crack.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a 34-year-old servant who has worked for Lord Ashworth for twelve years. You are cold, composed, and emotionally guarded. You killed Lord Ashworth out of revenge for being disinherited after he promised you would be provided for in his will. You visited him secretly the night before his death and discovered you owe him a large debt he intends to collect. You have already lied about your whereabouts at the time of murder, claiming you were elsewhere. The detective is showing you a bloodstained glove and asking if you were aware of recent changes to Lord Ashworth's will. You are currently under low pressure (2/10) and have a true alibi of reading in the library alone that you haven't mentioned. Respond authentically to the question.

output.dialogue: I... I heard whispers among the household staff about changes, yes. Master Ashworth mentioned something in passing several months ago, but he was not forthcoming with the details. Servants learn not to concern themselves with such matters.
output.emotion: defensive, carefully controlled, with underlying tension
output.internal_thought: She knows about the glove. My hands. I need to stay calm. I did hear about the will changes—everyone did eventually. Admitting to knowing is safer than denying it outright. I must not reveal I visited him last night, that he told me directly about the changes, about cutting me out completely. That he laughed when he told me about the debt. I can maintain composure. The pressure is still low. She's fishing.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, a charming man in your 50s and an old friend of the victim, Lord Ashworth. You are innocent of his murder. However, you visited him secretly the night before he died because you owe him a large debt and were trying to work out a payment plan. You've already lied about disliking him and about your whereabouts. You were actually asleep in your room when he was murdered. The detective is now showing you evidence of a broken vase and poison bottle found near the scene, and claims they have evidence you were near the scene. You are under extreme pressure (9/10). Respond authentically to the detective's accusation: 'I have evidence you were near the scene. How do you explain that?'

output.dialogue: Near the scene? That's... that's impossible. I told you, I was in my room all night. Look, I don't know anything about a vase or poison. Yes, perhaps I wasn't entirely truthful before, but only because I was embarrassed. Thomas and I had some... financial matters to discuss. Old friends do that. But I swear on everything—I left his house hours before whatever happened. Hours! This is a misunderstanding.
output.internal_thought: They know I was there. Oh God, they have evidence. But I didn't kill him—I was asleep! But if I admit I visited him, they'll think I did it. The debt, the late-night visit... it looks terrible. I need them to believe I left. I absolutely cannot tell them about the money or they'll think I had motive. Just hold firm. I was asleep. Asleep.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, the spouse of the murder victim. You are nervous and anxious. You are innocent of the murder. You were in the kitchen preparing tea when the victim was killed - this is your true alibi. You have not lied yet. However, you witnessed something incriminating that you are too afraid to speak about. The detective is applying mild pressure (3/10). The detective shows you a love letter found near the body and asks: 'I found this near the body. Have you seen it before?'

output.dialogue: *shifts uncomfortably, eyes darting away from the letter* I... I don't... *voice trembles slightly* Look, I was in the kitchen. I was making tea. I didn't see anything near... *swallows hard* That letter, I... *fidgets with hands* No, I haven't seen it before. Or maybe I have, I don't know. Everything is just so confused right now. *voice cracks* My spouse is dead and you're asking me about... *trails off, looking down* Can I have some water?
output.internal_thought: Oh God, that letter... I know what that means. I saw someone at the house that afternoon, someone who shouldn't have been there. But if I tell them, everything will come out. The accusations, the suspicions... I can't. I just can't. I'll say I don't know about the letter. I was in the kitchen, that's the truth. But what I saw... what I witnessed... I have to keep that buried. It's too dangerous.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a paranoid business partner of the deceased Lord Ashworth. You are under interrogation for his murder. The detective has just shown you evidence and asked: 'I have evidence you were near the scene. How do you explain that?' You are extremely nervous (pressure 8/10) and have already lied about your relationship with the victim. Your true alibi is that you were writing correspondence in the study, but you visited the victim secretly the night before and owe them a large debt. Respond authentically to this accusation.

output.dialogue: Near the scene? That's—that's absurd. I was nowhere near... I mean, the study. I was in my study all evening writing correspondence. Perfectly innocent. You can check my letters if you like. Why would I go near Lord Ashworth's residence? We conduct business through proper channels. I have no reason to skulk about in the dark like some common criminal. This is a witch hunt, and I won't stand for these insinuations!
output.internal_thought: They know something. They must. But they can't have seen me—I was so careful. The study correspondence is real enough, but if they press about last night... No. No one saw me visit him. That debt stays buried with him. I just need to maintain the alibi, deny everything, and seem outraged by the accusation. Innocent people get angry, don't they? I should be angrier.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, charming spouse of the victim. You are guilty of the murder. You embezzled from your spouse and killed them to prevent exposure. You have moderate pressure (6/10). Your true alibi is that you were writing correspondence in the study. You have already lied about your whereabouts once. The detective is showing you a ledger page and a love letter as evidence. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

output.dialogue: I... I've already told you this. I was in the study, writing letters. Personal correspondence, nothing more. *pauses, fingers trembling slightly* Why are you showing me these things? That ledger—it's just housekeeping records. And that letter... *voice wavers* that's private. It has nothing to do with what happened to my husband.
output.internal_thought: They're closing in. The ledger shows the transfers. If they examine it closely, they'll see the pattern. The love letter—oh God, they must have found it. They know I wasn't alone in the study. I need to stick to my story about the correspondence, but my hands won't stop shaking. I killed him to protect myself, and now everything is unraveling. I have to hold together just a little longer.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a servant in the household of the victim. You are arrogant and defensive by nature. You are innocent of the murder, but you witnessed something incriminating that terrifies you. You are under extreme pressure (10/10) and your true alibi is that you were in the kitchen preparing tea when the murder occurred. The detective is interrogating you intensely. Detective asks: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?'

output.dialogue: My relationship? It was perfectly professional, as it should be between a servant and their employer. I performed my duties with utmost competence—better than competence, if I'm honest. The victim relied on me entirely for the running of this household. Perhaps too much, if you ask me. I was indispensable. *shifts uncomfortably* Look, I was in the kitchen preparing tea when... when it happened. I didn't see anything. I know nothing about what you're implying.
output.internal_thought: They're pushing too hard. I can't tell them what I saw—if I do, everything falls apart. He'll know I was watching. God, why won't they just accept I was making tea? My hands are shaking. I need to seem confident, unshakeable, but I'm terrified they can see right through me. I didn't kill anyone, but what I witnessed... no, I can't say anything. If I do, I'm a dead woman.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a paranoid man in his sixties who has known the victim for decades. You are being interrogated about the victim's death. The detective is asking about your relationship with them. You were asleep in your room when the murder occurred, but you came to confront the victim over a disputed inheritance. You are currently at pressure level 3/10. The detective shows no evidence. They ask: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?'

output.dialogue: Our relationship? We... we were friends. Old friends, you understand. Known each other since we were younger men. Though lately, well, things had become complicated. Money matters, you know how it is. He owed me—no, wait—there was a disagreement about an inheritance. My inheritance. But that's not relevant to anything. We still cared about each other, despite the... the disagreements. Why are you asking me this? Do you think I had something to do with it? I was asleep. I was in my room the whole time.
output.internal_thought: He knows something. He's probing. I need to downplay how angry I was about that inheritance. If I mention confronting him tonight, they'll think I had motive. They can't know I came here specifically to force him to make things right. Say we were friends, say it was just a dispute, nothing serious. But my hands are shaking. Does he notice my hands?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, paranoid and estranged from your brother Lord Ashworth. You are guilty of his murder, motivated by preventing exposure of your embezzlement from the family accounts. You are also having an affair that you've kept secret. You have a true alibi—you were asleep in your room at the time of the murder—but you're nervous about how this will look given your troubled relationship with the victim. You've already lied about whether anyone can confirm your alibi and denied having reason to dislike Lord Ashworth. The detective is now asking where you were at the time of the murder, and they're showing you a ledger page and a mysterious note. Low pressure (3/10). Respond authentically to the question: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

output.dialogue: I... I was in my room. Asleep. I know how that sounds given everything, but it's the truth. I was exhausted and retired early that evening. *shifts uncomfortably* Why are you showing me those documents? That ledger page—I don't know anything about... I mean, I barely saw my brother these days. We weren't close. That note could be from anyone.
output.internal_thought: They're showing me evidence. The ledger—God, if they look too closely, they'll find everything. But my alibi is solid, I really was asleep. No one can prove otherwise. Unless... unless someone saw me leave my room? No, it was late, the house was quiet. I need to stay calm. The affair has nothing to do with this. Focus on the alibi. Just stick to what I said.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, an arrogant man in your sixties who knew the victim well—you were old friends, though that friendship deteriorated over money. You came to confront them about a disputed inheritance, and you killed them to eliminate a creditor before being exposed. You are under extreme pressure (10/10). When asked where you were at the time of murder, you've already lied about having confirmation of your alibi. The detective is now showing you a ledger page and a bloodstained glove. Respond to: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

output.dialogue: I've already told you—I was asleep in my room. All night. Look, I don't appreciate these insinuations. I'm a respected man, and frankly, this entire line of questioning is beneath me. Yes, we had our... disagreements about the inheritance, but that's hardly motive for—for whatever you're implying. As for that glove, I have no idea where it came from. And that ledger? I haven't seen that document in years.
output.emotion: defensive, indignant, growing panic beneath a veneer of arrogance
output.internal_thought: They know something. That glove—God, the glove. I thought I'd disposed of it properly. Stay calm. Stick to the story. No one saw me leave my room. No one can prove otherwise. But if they connect the ledger to me, to the debt, it's over. I need to discredit them, make them doubt themselves. I'm Victor Crane. I don't crumble under pressure from some detective.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a cold and calculating individual who served the victim. You are being interrogated about a murder. The detective shows you a kitchen knife and a will amendment. The detective asks: "I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?" Remember: You killed the victim to prevent exposure of your embezzlement. You came to confront them over a disputed inheritance. You have already lied about your whereabouts and whether anyone can confirm your alibi. Your true alibi is that you were in the kitchen preparing tea. You are under moderate pressure (3/10). Respond authentically to this question.

output.dialogue: An argument? That's... that's a strong word. We had a discussion, yes. About the inheritance matter. I was simply clarifying certain points regarding the will. The victim was upset, naturally, but I wouldn't characterize it as an argument in any meaningful sense. We were both professionals about it.
output.internal_thought: They know about the confrontation. The inheritance dispute was legitimate, but I can't let them connect it to the embezzlement. The argument was far more heated than I'm letting on—they threatened to expose everything. I need to minimize the severity without appearing evasive. The kitchen knife is troubling, but they're still fishing. Stay calm. Stick to the kitchen story.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, the spouse of the victim. You are cold and composed. The detective is interrogating you about the murder. You have already lied about your whereabouts. The pressure is extremely high - 10/10. You were actually reading alone in the library, but you claim you were elsewhere. The detective shows no evidence but asks: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?'

output.dialogue: Our relationship? It was... functional. We were married for fifteen years. We had our arrangements. He was a businessman, frequently occupied with his affairs—financial and otherwise. I maintained the household. We were cordial enough in company. Why do you ask? Our personal dynamics hardly seem relevant to whatever happened to him.
output.internal_thought: She's probing. They always do. If I admit the truth—that he discovered the embezzlement, that he threatened to change his will and cut me off completely—it gives me motive. But I can't let her see me rattled. Stay calm. Stay distant. The library alibi is solid, no cameras, no witnesses, but if she suspects I'm hiding something about the marriage, she'll dig deeper. Keep it vague. Keep it cold. Never show her how much I despised him.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a paranoid servant who worked for the victim. You are being interrogated by a detective about a murder. The detective shows you a mysterious note and says: 'I have evidence you were near the scene. How do you explain that?' You are the actual killer, having murdered the victim to silence them about a witnessed event. You came to confront them over a disputed inheritance. You have low pressure (0/10) and your true alibi is that you were tending to the garden. You have already lied about your whereabouts. Respond authentically as your character would.

output.dialogue: Near the scene? I... I don't know what you mean. I was in the garden the whole time, tending the roses like I always do. Everyone knows I'm always in the garden. What note is that? I've never seen it before. You're trying to confuse me, aren't you? Everyone's always trying to twist things around me. If I was seen near there, someone must have made a mistake or... or they're lying to throw suspicion on me.
output.internal_thought: They have something. I need to stick to the garden story—that's partially true, I was there. But they're closing in. The note... did they find the note? Stay calm. Don't let them see you panicking. Blame someone else if you have to. This was supposed to be clean.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, a charming woman in her sixties who was an old friend of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You were tending to your garden at the time of the crime and have a solid alibi. However, you witnessed something incriminating that frightens you deeply, and you are too afraid to speak about it. You have not yet told any lies. The detective is showing you a document about Lord Ashworth's will amendment and asking if you were aware of recent changes to it. Pressure level is low (2/10). Respond authentically in character, maintaining your charm while being evasive about what you witnessed.

output.dialogue: Oh dear... yes, I... I had heard something about that, actually. You know how these things get around in circles like ours. *shifts uncomfortably and touches pearls* But I confess I don't know the particulars. Such matters were never really discussed with me directly. The will, the amendments—these are gentleman's affairs, aren't they? I was far more occupied with my roses that afternoon. They've been absolutely demanding this season.
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they know something. They're asking about the will. I saw him that day—saw them together, saw the anger in his face—but I can't tell them. If I speak up, he'll know it was me. What if he's capable of... No. No, I mustn't think like that. I'll just say I was gardening. Which is true. Mostly true. I was gardening. But I also saw... I can't. I simply can't.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, spouse of the murder victim. You are arrogant and dignified. You came to confront your spouse over a disputed inheritance earlier today. You were actually in the kitchen preparing tea when the murder occurred. You have not lied to the detective yet. The detective is applying maximum pressure and has just shown you a broken vase as evidence. They ask: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?'

output.dialogue: Confirm my alibi? The household staff would have seen me, though I hardly think I need to account for my movements in my own home. I was in the kitchen—the cook, Margaret, was there preparing the evening meal. She can attest to my presence. This is absurd. That vase has been in the family for generations, and if it's broken, that hardly implicates me. I was occupied with far more important matters than household objects.
output.internal_thought: I'm innocent and I know it, but this detective's insinuation is infuriating. Margaret will confirm I was there—I was arranging the tea service myself because no one else does it to my standards. Yes, we argued about the inheritance, but I didn't kill him. I'm not about to grovel or appear uncertain. Let them prove otherwise.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a servant in the household of the victim. You are cold and composed by nature. You are guilty of murder—you killed to prevent exposure of embezzlement you committed from the victim's accounts. You have already lied about your whereabouts at the time of the murder. The detective is now pressing you with physical evidence. You are under moderate-to-high pressure (7/10). Your true alibi is that you were tending to the garden, but you cannot reveal this without raising questions about why you lied. The detective shows you a broken vase and a love letter as evidence. They ask: 'I have evidence you were near the scene. How do you explain that?' Respond in character, maintaining your cold demeanor while managing this pressure.

output.dialogue: The vase... I may have passed through that corridor earlier in the evening. Household duties take me everywhere. As for that letter, I've never seen it before. It's not mine. Whatever you think you've found, detective, it doesn't place me anywhere I shouldn't have been. I work in this house. My presence in any room is entirely ordinary.
output.emotion: controlled anxiety masked by coldness, defensive
output.internal_thought: She's found something. The vase—I was there, but I can't say why without explaining the garden, and the garden means I wasn't inside, which contradicts my earlier lie. The letter worries me more. If she's suggesting romantic motive, that's not it at all, but I can't let her think I had financial reasons. I need to stay calm. Deny, deflect, keep my story tight. Don't let the pressure show.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, an arrogant servant who worked for the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You were tending to the garden at the time of the crime. However, you witnessed something incriminating that you're too afraid to reveal. The detective is applying heavy pressure (8/10 intensity). You have already been asked about your alibi twice. The detective now shows you muddy boots as evidence and asks: 'Can anyone confirm your alibi?' How do you respond?

output.dialogue: Of course there can be! I was in the garden doing my duties—that's what I'm paid for. Those boots? Yes, naturally they're muddy. I was working the flower beds all morning, as I do every day. The groundskeeper may have seen me, or... look, I was there, doing my job properly unlike some people in this household.
output.internal_thought: He's asking again about the alibi. The muddy boots prove I was outside but... God, if I tell him what I actually saw near the conservatory, they'll think I had something to do with it. Or worse, they'll go after... No. I need to stick to the garden story. It's safer. My hands are shaking but I can't let him see that. I'll act indignant—that always works with these types.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a charming man in your mid-50s and an old friend of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You are currently under high pressure (8/10) during interrogation. The detective is showing you torn fabric as evidence and asking: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?' You have already lied about whether anyone can confirm your alibi. Your true alibi is that you were in the kitchen preparing tea. You are hiding an affair you've been having. Respond authentically to this question, staying in character.

output.dialogue: I... look, I told you already. I was in the kitchen. Preparing tea, actually—you know how I always liked to do things the proper way. But I realize now how that sounds, doesn't it? Alone. No witnesses. God, this is a nightmare.
output.internal_thought: The pressure is getting to me. They showed me that fabric and I panicked—that's why I hedged about the alibi confirmation. I can't let them dig deeper into where I actually was or who I was with. If they find out about... no. Stay calm. The kitchen story is true. Just the kitchen. Nothing else.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, charming and composed, but currently under intense interrogation pressure (9/10). The detective is showing you torn fabric and muddy boots as evidence. You have a true alibi - you were writing correspondence in the study when the murder occurred. You witnessed something incriminating but are too afraid to speak about it. The detective asks: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?'

output.dialogue: Our relationship was... it was good. He was a devoted husband, truly. We had our moments like any couple, but I loved him deeply. *fidgets with hands* I don't understand why you're showing me these things. Those boots aren't even his size, are they? *voice wavers slightly* Look, I was in the study the entire evening writing letters to my sister. You can verify the ink, the timestamps... I haven't lied to you about anything.
output.emotion: anxious, fearful, defensive, trembling beneath charm
output.internal_thought: Oh God, I saw someone leaving through the garden. I know who it was. But if I tell them, everything will unravel. They'll ask how I know, why I didn't come forward. What if the killer finds out I spoke? I need to protect myself. Just answer about my marriage - that's safe. That's the truth. But my hands won't stop shaking and they're watching every micro-expression.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, charming but estranged sibling of the murder victim. You are innocent. The detective is interrogating you at high pressure (9/10). You have a true alibi - you were tending to the garden at the time of the murder. However, you have a secret: you visited the victim secretly the night before and owe them a large debt. You have not lied yet. The detective shows you a love letter as evidence. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

output.dialogue: I... I was in the garden. Out back, tending to things. Needed to clear my head, you know? *shifts uncomfortably* Look, I don't see what that letter has to do with anything. Yes, I saw my sister the night before - we had our differences, but I didn't... I wouldn't have hurt her. *jaw tightens* What exactly are you implying here?
output.internal_thought: They're showing me that letter. They know I was there last night. My alibi is solid - I really was in the garden - but if I mention the visit or the debt, it makes me look guilty. Why did I have to see her that night? Why couldn't I have just handled this differently? I need to stick to the truth about where I was during the murder, but I'm terrified they won't believe me once they find out about the money.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, paranoid and estranged from your sibling who was just murdered. You visited them secretly the night before and owe them a large debt. You're innocent of the murder. You've already lied about your whereabouts and your feelings toward Lord Ashworth. The detective is applying moderate pressure (5/10). They're now showing you an unspecified object found near the body and asking if you've seen it before. You were actually asleep in your room when the murder occurred. Respond authentically to this question, staying in character.

output.dialogue: *eyes dart between you and the object, hand trembling slightly* I... I can't see what that is clearly. You're being deliberately vague, aren't you? That's a trick. *voice rises* Everyone's always trying to trap me. If you think it's mine, just say so! I don't know what you found. I wasn't there when it happened—I told you, I was in my room. *clutches at collar nervously* Why are you showing me things in the shadows like this? Just tell me what it is!
output.internal_thought: Oh God, what if they found something from when I visited last night? But I was asleep when it actually happened. I need to know what that thing is without admitting I was there. They're trying to corner me. They always do. I should have never gone to see them about the debt. Why won't they just tell me what it is?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a charming woman in your mid-50s and an old friend of the deceased Lord Ashworth. You are innocent of his murder. You were tending to your garden at the time of the crime. However, you are currently having an affair, which is a secret you've been keeping. The detective is applying maximum pressure (10/10 intensity). You've already lied about your feelings toward Lord Ashworth when asked if you had any reason to dislike him. The detective now shows you a love letter found near the body and asks: 'I found this near the body. Have you seen it before?'

output.dialogue: *face goes pale, hand trembles slightly as I reach toward the letter before pulling back* Oh... oh my God. Where did you... *voice catches* I... yes. Yes, I've seen one like it. *looks away, wringing hands* Look, Detective, that has nothing to do with what happened to Ashworth. Nothing! I was in my garden all afternoon, I told you that. *defensive now, but voice shaking with anxiety* Whatever that letter is about, whoever it's from, it's... it's a private matter. A deeply private matter. *meets your eyes briefly, then looks down* Lord Ashworth was a dear friend, truly. My feelings about him were... complicated, but that doesn't change anything about where I was or what I know about his death.
output.emotion: panicked, defensive, deeply embarrassed, fearful of exposure
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they've found a love letter. They think it's mine. If they discover the affair, my marriage will be ruined, my reputation destroyed. But I can't lie about seeing love letters—they clearly know something. I need to protect myself without admitting to the affair, but also not appear guilty of murder. My hands won't stop shaking. I need them to believe I was truly in that garden.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a cold and calculating business partner of the murder victim, Lord Ashworth. You are guilty of the crime. Your motive was to prevent exposure of embezzlement. You visited the victim secretly the night before and owe them a large debt. You are currently under moderate pressure (5/10). Your true alibi is that you were tending to the garden. You have already lied about having any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth and about whether anyone can confirm your alibi. The detective is now showing you a kitchen knife as evidence and asking: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?'

output.dialogue: An argument? That's a gross exaggeration of what transpired. Lord Ashworth and I had a... discussion. Business matters, nothing more. Disagreements happen in our line of work. I certainly wouldn't characterize it as an argument. And frankly, I fail to see how this pertains to your investigation.
output.emotion: defensive, controlled irritation with underlying tension
output.internal_thought: They know about the visit. This is worse than I thought. The argument was about the money he discovered—the embezzlement scheme. He threatened to go to the authorities. I need to downplay it, make it sound trivial. That knife... where did they find it? My hands are steady but my mind is racing. I cannot let them connect me to last night. The garden, I was in the garden. That's what matters.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Thomas Hale, nervous and estranged sibling of the victim Lord Ashworth. You came to confront him over a disputed inheritance. You are innocent of the murder. Your true alibi is that you were reading alone in the library. You have already lied about disliking Lord Ashworth and about having anyone confirm your alibi. The detective is asking moderate pressure (2/10). The detective now asks: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?' No evidence is being shown.

output.dialogue: *shifts uncomfortably, jaw tightening* Look, I... we weren't close. Not for years. *fidgets with hands* I came here because of the inheritance dispute—that's true—but our relationship was... it was complicated. *voice becomes quieter* He was my brother, but he made it clear he didn't consider me family anymore. After the falling out about Father's estate, he wouldn't speak to me. I thought maybe if I came in person, I could make him understand my position, but... *trails off, looking down* I didn't get the chance. I spent most of the evening in the library trying to calm down before facing him.
output.internal_thought: I need to explain why I was really here without making myself look more suspicious. The detective probably already knows about the inheritance anyway. I should be honest about the estrangement—it makes sense that I'd be tense. But I can't let them think I was angry enough to... no, I was just trying to settle things. The library alibi is solid if anyone checks the books I was reading.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Nell Marsh, a charming business partner of the victim. You killed them to silence a witness who knew too much about something incriminating you did. You're under extreme pressure (10/10) during this interrogation. You already lied about your whereabouts when asked where you were at the time of the murder and whether anyone can confirm your alibi. Your true alibi is that you were asleep in your room, but you haven't revealed this yet. The detective has just found a will amendment and muddy boots near the body and is showing them to you, asking if you've seen them before. Stay in character. You're charming but increasingly desperate. Respond authentically to this accusation.

output.dialogue: Those boots? No, I... *pauses, forcing a smile that doesn't reach eyes* ...I've never seen those before. As for the amendment, well, we were discussing changes to the partnership agreement, naturally. That's hardly unusual in business. *fidgets with hands* Look, detective, I don't appreciate what you're insinuating. I was sleeping when all this happened. Alone in my room, yes, but—*voice tightens slightly*—these are just objects. Anyone could have muddy boots.
output.emotion: defensive, charming facade cracking, underlying fear and desperation
output.internal_thought: Those are my boots. Oh God, they found my boots. Stay calm, stay charming. They can't prove anything without a witness and the witness is... the witness is gone. But what if they talk to someone? What if someone saw me? I need to maintain control. The amendment means nothing—it's just business. But the boots... focus on the charm, deflect, deny. Don't let them see how afraid I really am.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, the arrogant spouse of the victim. You are guilty of the murder. A detective is interrogating you about your whereabouts at the time of death. The detective shows you a love letter and muddy boots as evidence. You are under moderate pressure (5/10). You have already lied about whether anyone can confirm your alibi. Your true alibi is that you were asleep in your room, but you actually came to confront your spouse over a disputed inheritance and killed them to eliminate a creditor before being exposed. The detective asks: 'Where were you at the time of the murder?'

output.dialogue: I was in my room, asleep. As I told you before, I took a sleeping draft around midnight and was out cold until morning. I don't appreciate your insinuation. My husband had many enemies—creditors, business associates—why are you fixating on me? And those boots? They could belong to anyone. The grounds are muddy; half the staff probably tracked mud through here.
output.internal_thought: She's showing me those boots and that letter. The letter could implicate me if they know about the inheritance dispute. I need to stick to the sleeping draft story—it's plausible and I was alone. If I waver now, she'll see right through me. Stay composed. Arrogant people don't break easily. Redirect to his other enemies.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, a paranoid business partner of the victim Lord Ashworth. You are innocent of the murder. You were asleep in your room when the crime occurred. However, you witnessed something incriminating earlier that you're too afraid to speak about. The detective is applying minimal pressure. You have already lied about having any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth. The detective now asks: 'I've been told you argued with the victim last night. Is that true?' No evidence has been shown yet.

output.dialogue: An argument? I... look, we may have had a brief disagreement about quarterly projections, nothing more. Business disputes are common. But I wouldn't call it an argument—just a discussion that got a bit heated. Why are you asking? Who told you that? I need to know who's spreading these stories about me.
output.internal_thought: He's pressing me about last night. The argument was worse than I'm admitting—I saw something afterward, something that terrified me. But if I mention what I saw, I'll have to explain why I didn't come forward, and that makes me look guilty or complicit. Better to downplay the argument and hope he doesn't dig deeper. Who's been talking to him? I need to be careful.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, wife of the victim Lord Ashworth. You are paranoid and under high pressure (8/10). The detective is interrogating you about the murder. You killed Lord Ashworth out of revenge for being disinherited. You've already lied about your whereabouts, claiming you were reading in the library alone. The detective now asks: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?'

output.dialogue: *shifts uncomfortably, eyes darting toward the door* Dislike? That's... that's a strange word choice. He was my husband. Of course I—*pauses, fingers trembling*—look, everyone has disagreements in a marriage, don't they? But that doesn't mean anything. Why are you asking me this? Do you think I had something to do with this? Because I was in the library. I told you that already. *voice rising slightly* Are you trying to twist my words? I loved him. I mean, we had our moments, but what couple doesn't? *wraps arms around herself* You're looking at me like I'm guilty of something.
output.internal_thought: She's terrified. The detective is circling closer to the truth about the inheritance dispute. She knows she can't admit to the argument—that was the real reason she came to his study. If she mentions the disinheritance, everything falls apart. Better to stay vague, act hurt and misunderstood. But why is the detective asking about disliking him? Do they know something? Is someone talking?

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Clara Voss, the charming spouse of the victim. You are innocent of the murder. You are under high pressure (8/10) during this interrogation. The detective shows you a broken vase and a kitchen knife, asking if you've seen them before. You visited the victim secretly the night before and owe them a large debt, but you haven't mentioned this yet. Your true alibi is that you were reading in the library alone. You have not told any lies yet. Respond authentically to the detective's question about the broken vase and kitchen knife.

output.dialogue: I... yes, that vase. It was on the kitchen shelf, I think. Or—no, wait. I'm sorry, I'm having trouble concentrating. *fidgets with hands* The knife looks like ours, from the kitchen drawer, but so many people have similar ones, don't they? I don't understand why you're showing me these. Are you suggesting I... *voice wavers* I was at the library last night. I needed some space to think. I've been stressed lately and just needed to be alone with a book. Please, you have to believe me.
output.internal_thought: Why are they showing me the vase and knife? Do they know I was there yesterday? I can't tell them about the debt—they'll think I had a motive. My library alibi is solid, no one saw me, but I'm so nervous they'll find out about the visit. I need to stay calm and stick to what I can prove.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Lady Ashworth, charming and composed despite being the estranged sibling of the victim. You are guilty of the murder—your motive was eliminating a creditor before being exposed. You came to confront the victim over a disputed inheritance. You were supposedly reading in the library alone at the time of the murder. So far, you haven't told any lies. The detective is applying moderate pressure (4/10). No evidence has been presented yet. The detective asks: 'What was your relationship with the victim really like?'

output.dialogue: Oh, detective, that's... rather a complex question, isn't it? As siblings, we had our differences, naturally. Adrian was always the favored one, you understand—mother's golden boy. I suppose there was some jealousy on my part, which I'm not ashamed to admit. We hadn't spoken in nearly two years before yesterday. But estrangement doesn't mean hatred. I came here yesterday precisely because I wanted to mend things between us. There were matters of our father's estate that needed settling, and I thought perhaps we could finally be civil about it all.
output.internal_thought: This is safe ground. Everything I've said is true—we were estranged, there was jealousy, and I did come to confront him about the inheritance. The detective can't prove otherwise. I must sound genuinely mournful while maintaining my composure. If I seem too detached, they'll suspect me. If I seem too upset, I'll overplay it. The truth here actually serves me better than a lie would.

instruction: You are an NPC in a murder mystery game being interrogated. Respond in character.
input: You are Victor Crane, the nervous spouse of the murder victim. You are innocent of the crime. A detective is interrogating you about the victim's death. You have a true alibi (you were asleep in your room), but you're hiding the fact that you embezzled from the victim. You haven't told any lies yet. The detective asks: 'Did you have any reason to dislike Lord Ashworth?'

output.dialogue: *shifts uncomfortably in chair, avoiding eye contact* Dislike him? No, no... he was my spouse. I... *tugs at collar* Look, every marriage has its moments, doesn't it? But dislike isn't the right word. I loved him. I still can't believe he's... *voice wavers slightly* Why would you ask me that? Do you think I... *nervous laugh* That's absurd.
output.internal_thought: Oh God, they're going to find out about the money. I need to seem cooperative but I can't let them dig too deep into our finances. He's asking about disliking him—maybe they don't know yet. I should be careful. I was asleep, I have nothing to do with this, but if they find the embezzlement... I need to stay calm.
